title: My Dog Always Eats First: Homeless People and Their Animals By Leslie Irvine Lynne Rienner Publishers. 2012. 197 pages. $55.00
abstract: s someone who enjoyed Professor Irvine's previous work on human-animal relations, I very much looked forward to reading this book, and I will say from the outset that it did not disappoint. This is a wide-ranging book that addresses many aspects of the challenges faced by homeless people who opt to live with animals. It is based largely on interviews with 75 people identifying as homeless in Colorado, California, and Florida in the United States. Access was facilitated through various NGOs and individuals who worked with homeless people and their animals. The author makes it clear that she is adopting a narrative approach to the study of homeless people and their companion animals. By doing this, she aims to "honor" how those interviewed for the research "construct the stories they tell about themselves and their relationships with their animals" (6). And she remains faithful to this throughout the book, which is, in large part, what makes it so captivating and easy to read. But I am getting a little ahead of myself here. Irvine opens the book with a story from her own life; a brave story, I believe. She recounts how she once approached a homeless person to ask about the welfare of his dog and how, ultimately, she and a colleague from the local animal shelter offered to buy the dog from the young homeless man. It was his response-that his dog "has a great life. He runs around in the forest all the time. He never has to be on a leash. . .He's got food. He's got water. He's had his shots. He never leaves my side. He's fine" (2)-that prompted Irvine to think about the issues that lie at the heart of the current book. I mark this story as brave because it demonstrates what I believe to be a typical response to homeless (or any other marginalized) people who live with animals, and in many ways it is a very privileged, Western response. In one respect, then, it is heartening to see the author begin to change her perspective as she recounts the events that lead up to the fieldwork for this book, and then to see these beliefs further refined and altered through her actual engagement with homeless people and their animals. To my mind, this is a key strength of this work-it challenges
